flashcards.front: What is storage management in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Storage management refers to the OS function of controlling and coordinating computer memory allocation, ensuring efficient use of both primary (RAM) and secondary storage (disks, SSDs) resources.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between primary storage and secondary storage?
flashcards.back: Primary storage (RAM) is fast, volatile memory directly accessed by CPU. Secondary storage (HDD, SSD) is slower, non-volatile memory used for permanent data retention.
A memory allocation method where each process is assigned a single continuous block of memory. Simple but leads to external fragmentation and difficulty in dynamic memory expansion.
External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks throughout RAM, making it impossible to allocate a large process despite sufficient total free memory.
Internal fragmentation is wasted memory within an allocated block due to rounding up of allocation requests to fixed units (e.g., pages or fixed-size partitions).

flashcards.front: What is paging in memory management?
flashcards.back: Paging divides both physical memory and logical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames and pages respectively. It eliminates external fragmentation but may cause internal fragmentation.
A page table is a data structure maintained by the OS that maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers, enabling virtual memory to appear as contiguous memory to processes.
Virtual memory is a memory management technique that allows execution of processes that may not be completely in RAM by using disk space as temporary storage (swap space).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: TLB is a special cache that stores recent page table translations, providing fast virtual-to-physical address translation and reducing memory access time.
Thrashing occurs when the OS spends excessive time swapping pages in and out of memory due to insufficient frames, causing severe performance degradation.
FIFO (First In First Out), Optimal (OPT), LRU (Least Recently Used), LFU (Least Frequently Used), and Clock (Second Chance) algorithms.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model?
flashcards.back: The working set model defines the set of pages a process is actively using. The OS ensures enough frames are allocated to keep the working set in memory to minimize thrashing.
Disk scheduling is the process of determining the order in which disk I/O requests are served to minimize seek time, rotational latency, and improve throughput.

flashcards.front: What is the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm?
flashcards.back: SCAN (also called elevator algorithm) moves the disk head in one direction servicing all requests until it reaches the end, then reverses direction to service remaining requests.
Swap space is reserved disk space used by the OS to extend RAM when it becomes full. The OS moves inactive pages to swap space (paging out) and brings them back when needed (paging in).
